display_name: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan (joint interview)
role_summary: Joint oral history of Guy Carawan (1927-2015) and Candie Anderson Carawan, the Highlander Folk School music teachers who taught "We Shall Overcome" to SNCC and SCLC organizers in 1960 and shaped the corpus of movement freedom songs.
ai_reading: The Carawans' top semantic neighbor is Scott Bates (entry 116, count 118), the SCLC Educational Director cohort member; second is Jamila Jones (entry 58, count 81), one of the singers who learned the freedom-song corpus at Highlander; third is Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29, SCLC Educational Director); fourth is Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7, SNCC Northern support); fifth is Pete Seeger (entry 97, count 42). The Highlander music-tradition cluster the catalog has now documented across multiple entries (Pete Seeger, Jamila Jones, Scott Bates, and the Carawans themselves) is at its tightest here, the Carawans as the central node of the triadic-plus-Bates-plus-Seeger freedom-song-pedagogy signature. Their axis positions are all moderate (range -0.18 to +0.41), placing them in the catalog's centrist-voice band.

photo.alt: Guy Carawan, folk musician and Highlander Folk School music director

interview_snippets.speaker: Candie Carawan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Candie Carawan, who had come up from the Nashville sit-in movement, on what the Nashville students had sung before the April 1960 SNCC founding gathering at Highlander and what Guy Carawan taught them there:
interview_snippets.quote: But we didn't have what I would call freedom songs. And he taught us, I'm going to stay at the left and table, keep your eyes on the prize and we shall overcome.

interview_snippets.speaker: Guy Hughes Carawan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked by the interviewer whether driving Septima Clark around for Highlander felt like a good assignment, Guy Carawan answers:
interview_snippets.quote: No, that was incredible to be able to meet her driver. I just calm down and listen to my superior. I went to speed up and went to slow down. I love her.

interview_snippets.speaker: Candie Carawan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Candie Carawan recounts how Pete Seeger first pointed Guy Carawan toward Highlander when Guy wanted to travel the South his parents came from:
interview_snippets.quote: Pete is the one that told Guy about Highlander. Guy told Pete he wanted to travel around the South and see where his parents had grown up. Charleston South Carolina for his mother. rural North Carolina for his father and he said, oh, it's a great idea for you to travel a South.

interview_snippets.speaker: Candie Carawan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Candie Carawan on the two-way exchange Guy Carawan ran when civil rights organizers came to the Highlander workshops:
interview_snippets.quote: He was the one that was invited out to Southern communities. As people came to Highlander to workshops about the civil rights movement, Guy would be there to teach songs that he knew but also learn songs that people brought with him.

interview_snippets.speaker: Guy Hughes Carawan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Guy Carawan on why a song-leader's repertoire mattered to communities that had been through hard experiences:
interview_snippets.quote: But you needed people who helped a lot to have people who could play guitars or banjos or no songs, have a repertoire of songs that would say how you felt or how a group of people have gone through bad experiences could make, try to make some good out of it, and freedom songs, and standing together, making use of the songs.

interview_snippets.speaker: Candie Carawan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Candie Carawan quotes John Lewis on the place of music in the movement:
interview_snippets.quote: I liked it when John Lewis said, well, the movement without its music would be like a bird without its wing.

interview_snippets.speaker: Jamila Jones
interview_snippets.lead_in: Jamila Jones, who learned the freedom-song corpus as a teenager singing with the Montgomery group at Highlander, on the workshops where the songs began to change:
interview_snippets.quote: I do want to say a bit more about how Linda and how our songs began to change as we attended those workshops with Guy Carawan and all.

biographical_paragraph: This is a joint oral history at the Library of Congress as entry 11 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1]. Joint interviews in the corpus combine the testimony of two or more interviewees in a single recording; the catalog page treats the embedding signature of the joint transcript as one unit (see the AI's reading above). The joint Civil Rights History Project oral history of Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan (joint interview) is cross-referenced against the Library of Congress published transcript.
movement_context: The Highlander Folk School at Monteagle, Tennessee, founded in 1932 by Myles Horton and Don West as an adult-education center for Southern labor organizing, became the principal interracial training ground of the southern civil rights movement under Horton's direction across the 1950s; Rosa Parks attended a Highlander workshop on school desegregation in late July 1955, four months before her December 1, 1955 Montgomery bus arrest [src: 2]. Guy Carawan, who had toured the South in 1953 with Frank Hamilton and Ramblin' Jack Elliott studying African American folk-music traditions, joined Highlander as music director in 1959 [src: 2]. The April 15-17, 1960 SNCC founding meeting at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina was where Carawan introduced "We Shall Overcome" (the version he had adapted from the labor-movement hymn the song was descended from, through Zilphia Horton's 1947 Highlander adaptation and Pete Seeger's 1948 fingerstyle arrangement) to the student-organizer cohort that would carry it through the next decade as the movement's anthem [src: 3]. The song's status as the freedom-song corpus's most-circulated piece is sourced through Carawan's pedagogy: he and Candie Anderson Carawan (a Pomona College student who had been at the February 1960 Nashville sit-ins and joined him at Highlander after their 1961 marriage) ran the Highlander music workshops that taught the song-leading pedagogy to the SNCC field workers who in turn taught it to local Mississippi, Alabama, and Georgia movement participants [src: 3].
legacy_and_reception: Tennessee revoked Highlander's charter in 1961 (following a long state harassment campaign that included a 1957 photograph of Martin Luther King Jr. at a Highlander workshop circulated by segregationists as alleged communist-subversion evidence); Horton refounded the institution as the Highlander Research and Education Center in 1961 and moved operations to Knoxville (later New Market, Tennessee) where it operates today [src: 4]. The Carawans' 1990 University Press of Mississippi reissue of their 1968 collection Sing for Freedom: The Story of the Civil Rights Movement Through Its Songs and Lyrics is the canonical published record of the freedom-song corpus and the Highlander pedagogy that taught it; the volume contains lyrics, sheet music, and oral-history commentary on roughly seventy songs the southern movement carried [src: 5]. Guy Carawan died May 2, 2015 at eighty-seven at his home in New Market, Tennessee, where he and Candie had lived since the Highlander relocation [src: 6].

gallery.alt: Highlander Folk School historical marker, Monteagle, Tennessee
gallery.caption: Tennessee Historical Commission marker at the original Highlander Folk School site near Monteagle, Tennessee. The marker documents the school's 1932-1962 institutional history, including the visits of Martin Luther King Jr., Rosa Parks, and John Lewis, and Tennessee's 1961 revocation of the school's charter. Highlander refounded as the Highlander Research and Education Center the same year and continues to operate.

gallery.alt: Highlander Folk School near Monteagle, Tennessee, late 1930s
gallery.caption: A scene at the original Highlander Folk School near Monteagle, Tennessee, photographed by Swiss writer and photographer Annemarie Schwarzenbach (1908-1942) on her 1936-1938 American tour. The photograph predates the Carawans' tenure by twenty years; the institutional context Schwarzenbach documented (an adult-education center for Southern labor organizing, founded 1932 by Myles Horton and Don West) is the program Horton would re-direct toward civil rights training across the 1950s. Held by the Swiss Literary Archives, Swiss National Library.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Swiss National Library, Swiss Literary Archives)
gallery.license: Public domain (author died 1942; copyright term 70 years post-mortem)
